translatedText: Died in Essex.
Pioneer of British Empiricism
Considered the first major British empiricist philosopher.
Proposing the Social Contract Theory
Argued that the state emerged through a social contract to ensure the security of natural rights.
Enlightenment and Liberal Thinker
Widely known as one of the most influential Enlightenment thinkers and liberal theorists.
Origin of the 'Self-Identity' Concept
The first philosopher to define the self through a continuity of 'consciousness'.
Advocating the 'Blank Slate' (Tabula Rasa)
Argued that people are born without innate ideas, treating the mind as a 'blank slate'.
Proposing the Night-Watchman State Theory
Advocated for the night-watchman state, arguing that the duty of the state lies in guaranteeing minimal security.
Laying the foundation for Popular Sovereignty
Laid the foundation for popular sovereignty by arguing that the people entrusted power to the state through a contract.
Advocating Constitutional Democracy and Natural Rights
The first to advocate for constitutional democratic government and natural rights.
Laying the foundation for the Separation of Powers
Created the foundation for the separation of powers into the legislative, executive, and judicial branches.
Authorship of 'An Essay Concerning Human Understanding'
His main work addressing the problem of cognition, including modern science.
Authorship of 'The Reasonableness of Christianity'
A text written as a response to John Toland's draft.
Authorship of 'A Letter Concerning Toleration'
Argued that "toleration is indeed the most evident mark of a true church."
How is John Locke evaluated as a philosopher?
Locke is evaluated as the first major British empiricist philosopher, and is known as one of the most influential Enlightenment thinkers and liberal theorists.
What are John Locke's main works?
His main works include 'An Essay Concerning Human Understanding', 'Two Treatises of Government', 'The Reasonableness of Christianity', and 'A Letter Concerning Toleration'.
Who did John Locke's ideas influence?
Locke's ideas influenced Voltaire, Rousseau, the American Revolution, and Scottish Enlightenment thinkers, and are also reflected in the American Declaration of Independence.
What is the 'blank slate' that John Locke argued for?
Locke regarded the mind as a 'blank slate' (tabula rasa), arguing that people are born without innate ideas.
What does John Locke's social contract theory argue?
Locke argued that the state was formed by a social contract to ensure the security of natural rights, such as holding one's own property through labor in a peaceful state of nature.
What was John Locke's position on religious toleration?
Although Locke argued that "toleration is indeed the most evident mark of a true church," he showed a contradictory attitude by excluding Roman Catholics and atheists from the objects of toleration.
What was John Locke's position on slavery?
Locke opposed slavery in England, but he showed a contradictory attitude by condoning the ownership of black slaves by Englishmen in the American colonies.
Simone de Beauvoir was born in Paris, France.
Presented on Leibniz at the Sorbonne and passed the philosophy agrégation exam in second place.
Published the novel 'She Came to Stay' (L'Invitée).
Wrote the essay on existentialist ethics, 'Pyrrhus and Cineas' (Pyrrhus et Cinéas).
Published 'The Ethics of Ambiguity' (Pour Une Morale de L'ambiguïté).
'The Second Sex' (Le Deuxième Sexe) was published in France.
Published the autobiography 'Memoirs of a Dutiful Daughter' (Mémoires d'une jeune fille rangée).
Published the autobiography 'The Prime of Life' (La Force de l'âge).
Published the autobiography 'Force of Circumstance' (La Force des choses).
Wrote the prose work 'The Coming of Age' (La Vieillesse).
Signed the Manifesto of the 343, advocating for the legalization of abortion.
Published the autobiography 'All Said and Done' (Tout compte fait).
Published 'When Things of the Spirit Come First' (Quand prime le spirituel), a collection of stories about her experiences as a woman in her youth.
Wrote 'Adieux: A Farewell to Sartre' (La Cérémonie Des Adieux) covering Sartre's later years.
Died of pneumonia and was buried in Montparnasse Cemetery, Paris.
Author and Philosopher
A prominent French author and philosopher who wrote across various genres including novels, philosophical essays, treatises, biographies, and autobiographies.
'She Came to Stay'
A fictionalized chronicle of her complex relationship with Sartre, Olga Kosakiewicz, and Wanda Kosakiewicz.
'The Mandarins'
Set after World War II, this Prix Goncourt-winning novel portrays people around her, including Sartre and Nelson Algren.
'The Second Sex'
A monumental work providing an in-depth analysis of women's oppression, becoming the cornerstone of modern feminism.
'The Ethics of Ambiguity'
An accessible introduction to French existentialism, possessing a simplicity that contrasts with Sartre's more obscure writings.
Founding and editing 'Les Temps Modernes'
Co-founded the political journal 'Les Temps Modernes' with Sartre, Merleau-Ponty, and others, and worked as an editor until her death.
Authorship of autobiographies
Documented her life through four renowned autobiographies, beginning with 'Memoirs of a Dutiful Daughter'.
Participation in the French Women's Liberation Movement
Actively participated in the French women's liberation movement in the 1970s and signed the Manifesto of the 343, advocating the legalization of abortion.
Who was Simone de Beauvoir?
She was a French writer and philosopher who wrote novels as well as philosophical, political, and social essays, treatises, biographies, and autobiographies.
What are her representative works?
She wrote metaphysical novels such as 'She Came to Stay' and 'The Mandarins', and in 1949 wrote 'The Second Sex', a foundational text of modern feminism.
What was Simone de Beauvoir's family background?
She was born to Georges de Beauvoir, a legal secretary, and Françoise Brasseur. Her younger sister, Hélène de Beauvoir, was a painter.
What was her relationship with Jean-Paul Sartre?
She met Sartre at the University of Paris. In the 1929 philosophy agrégation exam, Sartre ranked first and she ranked second, and they maintained a complex relationship throughout their lives.
What is the core argument she made in 'The Second Sex'?

She argued that men had instilled a false aura of 'mystery' in women, turning them into the social 'Other', and embracing the maxim 'existence precedes essence', she famously posited that one is not born, but rather becomes, a woman.
How did she participate in the women's liberation movement?

She was active in the French women's liberation movement in the 1970s, and in 1971 she signed the Manifesto of the 343, which demanded the legalization of abortion, which was illegal in France at the time.
Born in Linden, Germany.
Published her dissertation on the concept of love.
Prevented from acquiring a teaching qualification for being Jewish, she fled Germany for Paris.
Married Heinrich Blücher.
Fled to the United States with her husband and mother.
Became a naturalized U.S. citizen.
Published 'The Origins of Totalitarianism'.
Published 'Rahel Varnhagen: The Life of a Jewish Woman' and 'The Human Condition'.
Became the first woman appointed to a full professorship at Princeton University.
Published 'Between Past and Future'.
Published 'Eichmann in Jerusalem: A Report on the Banality of Evil' and 'On Revolution'.
Published 'Men in Dark Times'.
Published 'Crises of the Republic: Lying in Politics, Civil Disobedience on Violence, Thoughts on Politics, and Revolution'.
Published the anthology 'Crises of the Republic'.
Passed away.
Her unfinished posthumous work 'The Life of the Mind' was published.
Establishment of identity as a political theorist
Describing herself as a political theorist rather than a philosopher, she left behind a legacy of work concerning humanity's inhabitation of the world.
Research on power, politics, authority, and totalitarianism
She made significant contributions exploring the nature of power, politics, authority, and topics such as totalitarianism.
Theorization of the concept of freedom
Theorized freedom as public and associative, focusing on affirming freedom as collective political action.
Presentation of the concept of the 'banality of evil'
In her report on the Eichmann trial, she concluded that sheer thoughtlessness—an inability to think—ultimately leads to the banality of evil.
Authorship of 'The Origins of Totalitarianism'
Wrote a book tracing the roots of communism and Nazism and their connection to anti-Semitism.
Authorship of 'On Revolution'
Compared the American and French Revolutions, arguing for the success of the American Revolution and the disaster of the French Revolution.
Establishment of the theory of power
Defined power as 'never the property of an individual, belonging to a group and remaining in existence only so long as the group keeps together', strictly distinguishing it from violence.
How did Hannah Arendt define herself?
Arendt herself rejected the title of philosopher on the grounds that philosophy is concerned with 'man in the singular', describing herself instead as a political theorist.
What is the 'banality of evil' proposed by Arendt?
In her report on the Eichmann trial, Arendt concluded that sheer thoughtlessness—an inability to think—ultimately breeds the banality of evil.
What is Arendt's concept of power?
Arendt defined power as 'never the property of an individual; it belongs to a group and remains in existence only so long as the group keeps together', strictly contrasting it with violence.
